source.title: Does Working From Home Work? Evidence from a Chinese Experiment
source.cite: Bloom, Liang, Roberts & Ying · Stanford / Ctrip · 2015

annotations.quote: Home working led to a 13% improvement in performance
annotations.note: Headline positive result speaks directly to the root question.

annotations.quote: handling more calls per minute in a quieter, more convenient environment
annotations.note: Gain attributed specifically to focused solo call-handling.

annotations.quote: Yet when later given the choice, over half asked to return to the office — and the gains for that group narrowed once they did.
annotations.note: Suggests the short-term gain may not persist — the time-horizon distinction matters.

annotations.quote: The setting is a specific one: routine, individually-measured call-centre work, where output is unusually easy to quantify.
annotations.note: Population is narrow (call-centre staff); limits who the result covers.

annotations.quote: The setting is a specific one: routine, individually-measured call-centre work, where output is unusually easy to quantify.
annotations.note: Task type is narrow (routine, measurable); may not generalise to creative/collaborative work.
